source: Mining mechanics & fits: community-sourced (SC Wiki, UEX, community guides) + adversarially verified against the gear catalog. Gear specs from data/mining-gear.json.
caveats: Mining is skill + rock-dependent: these fits are starting points — read each rock's resistance/instability on the scan and adjust.
Mineral prices fluctuate constantly; the value tiers are rough. Check a live tool (UEX / Regolith) before a sell run.
Module/head availability & stats shift between patches. Verify in-game. Unofficial — not affiliated with Cloud Imperium Games.

loop.title: Prospect & Scan
loop.detail: Locate mining sites and analyze rock composition, resistance, and instability before fracturing.

loop.title: Fracture
loop.detail: Charge the rock using your laser, holding the throttle in the green optimal charge window until fracture, without overcharging (red zone).

loop.title: Extract
loop.detail: Once fractured, ore falls into cargo. Manage inert material to maximize yield per SCU.

loop.title: Haul, Refine & Sell
loop.detail: Return to refinery, process ore into refined materials over time, then trade for UEC at commodity markets.

stats.name: Resistance
stats.what: How hard the rock is to break; how much laser power struggle to penetrate

stats.name: Instability
stats.what: How violently the charge bar jumps during fracturing; risk of overcharge explosion

stats.name: Optimal Charge Window
stats.what: Size of the green 'sweet spot' you must hold throttle in

stats.name: Charge Rate (Optimal)
stats.what: How fast you fill the optimal window
stats.better: higher = faster fracture, more per-hour yield

stats.name: Overcharge Rate
stats.what: How fast you approach the red danger zone once past optimal
stats.better: lower = more margin for error, safer

stats.name: Inert Materials
stats.what: Worthless rock material collected alongside ore; wastes cargo space

moduleTypes.passive: Always active once installed; permanent stat adjustments (e.g., Arbor heads -35% instability, Okunis +30% window, Optimum -80% overcharge danger). No cooldown.
moduleTypes.active: Manually triggered via hotkey for brief duration with limited uses per fracture (3–5 uses). Activated mid-fracture for reactive boosts (stabilize spikes, speed charging). Then cooldown.
moduleTypes.gadget: Single-use item consumed per deposit. Attach before fracturing for one-time bonus (Sabir -resistance, WaveShift +window). Must replace after use.

tips: Always scan rocks before committing. Skip those with >30% inert or resistance you cannot overcome with your equipped gear.
Hunt naturally low-instability rocks as a beginner. Stable deposits train throttle control and build confidence faster.
Build your laser with passive modules matching your playstyle: maximize window size for safety, or stack charge rate for speed. Never fly unmodded.
Save gadgets (consumable single-use) for hard rocks (high resistance, high instability). Do not waste them on easy deposits.
Master throttle control: smooth, steady input beats aggressive input. If the bar drifts toward red, pull back. If stalled in yellow, nudge up gently.
Watch overcharge margin on volatile rocks. Use overcharge-reducing gear (Optimum, Forel) to give yourself breathing room against fast-spiking deposits.
Manage cargo for profit. Inert material steals space directly: 100 SCU hold at 20% inert = only 80 SCU real ore. Use FLTR to filter inert if profitable, or target naturally low-inert rocks <15%.
mechanics.refining: After extraction, ore must be refined at a refinery to become sellable materials. Refining earns profit: raw ore sells cheaper than refined ore, so the refinery's processing time and yield percentage create the margin. Different ore types have different refining times and yields. Refining is the profit multiplier—not the focus of ship mining gameplay, but critical to overall earnings."

oreTiers.note: ~150K UEC/SCU, highest value but volatile — decays if improperly stored
~27.5K UEC/SCU, rarer than Laranite, better margin
~4.6K UEC/SCU, cheapest, abundant — workhorse for bulk runs

spots.what: Quantanium, Bexalite, Taranite — high-value belt, medium to large ships
Quantanium, Borase, Laranite — excellent for Prospector/MOLE
Agricium, Laranite, Titanium — ship + cave mining
Hadanite, Dolivine, Aphorite — ROC surface mining
Hadanite, Dolivine, Aphorite — harsh conditions, ROC mining effective
Copper, Laranite, Borase, Stileron — high risk/reward, pirates present

ships.name: MISC Prospector
ships.note: The solo mining workhorse. One swappable S1 mining head; module slots depend on the head you fit.

fits.tagline: Wide, forgiving window with maximum clean ore retention
fits.why: Impact I has 2 module slots, both filled here. Focus II adds Optimal Charge Window +37% and XTR-XL adds Optimal Charge Window +25% plus All Charge Rates +6 and Inert Materials +6 — stacking to a very wide, very forgiving green band so you sit at near-optimal charge without overcharging, while the inert reduction keeps cargo dense with sellable ore. OptiMax gadget cuts valuable-resource loss on the deposit. The whole stack pulls the two yield levers: widen the window (Focus II +37 / XTR-XL +25) and minimize inert (XTR-XL / OptiMax).
fits.playstyle: Scan for high-value deposits, attach OptiMax, then hold the laser comfortably in the very wide green band. Because the window is huge you can run near-optimal charge for long stretches without risking overcharge, maximizing extracted mass while inert reduction keeps the cargo dense with real ore.
fits.bestFor: Quantanium, Bexalite, Taranite and other high-value ore where every percent of clean yield matters; medium-resistance rocks you can crack without resistance gear.

fits.tagline: Resistance shredding plus overcharge control for the toughest deposits
fits.why: Cracking high-resistance rock needs resistance reduction plus power control. Helix I brings 2 module slots and reduces inert as a bonus. Rime (active) delivers Resistance -25% and Shatter Damage -10%, your primary cracking tool. Optimum (active) gives Instability -10 and Overcharge Rate -80%, so when you pour full power into a stubborn rock you do not run away into the explosion zone. The Sabir gadget modulates laser power to make a tough deposit easier to crack, layering more effective resistance reduction on top of Rime. Net: Rime -25% resistance + Sabir + Optimum's -80% overcharge = you break rocks other Prospector fits cannot while staying safe at full power.
fits.playstyle: Attach Sabir to the tough deposit, trigger Optimum first to flatten overcharge, then trigger Rime as you ramp full power to punch through the resistance threshold. Re-trigger Rime on big multi-charge rocks; fall back on Optimum for the riskiest high-power moments.
fits.bestFor: Dense high-resistance C-type and hardened deposits, large boulders, and any rock a single laser normally bounces off; experienced hand who manages the window closely.

fits.tagline: The calm-rock fit: low instability, an enormous window
fits.why: The Arbor MH1 is the textbook safety head: it reduces Laser Instability (rocks barely fluctuate, far less overcharge risk), widens the Optimal Charge Window, and lowers inert for decent yield. It has only 1 slot, spent on Focus III for Optimal Charge Window +40%, stacking to a huge green band that is almost impossible to miss. The Waveshift gadget is the perfect partner: it keeps instability in check while increasing the size of the optimal charge window, so on top of the head's low instability and the wide window you get an even calmer, even wider target. This fit removes the two things that kill the rock for new miners: violent instability and a window too small to hold.
fits.playstyle: Scan a low-to-medium rock, attach Waveshift, and slowly ramp the laser into the giant green band. You can sit there comfortably and let it fill with almost no fear of overcharging out. Ideal for learning the rhythm of charge and extract before moving to tighter expert heads.
fits.bestFor: New miners, calm low-instability deposits, and grinding common ore safely; also a reliable solo workhorse loadout when you just want dependable cycles.

fits.tagline: Maxed charge rate — speed-mine a field of soft rocks
fits.why: Lancet MH1 is the speed head with high Optimal Charge Rate and reduced inert, but a punishing narrow Optimal Charge Window that makes it expert-only. With its single slot we double down on speed using Torrent III: Optimal Charge Rate +40% for very fast fills at the cost of only Optimal Charge Window -1% (negligible versus Torrent's -10%). To offset the brutal narrow window without sacrificing rate, we bring the Okunis gadget, which increases the size and fill speed of the optimal charge window — so the deposit becomes both faster and far more forgiving than the bare head, letting you actually exploit the high charge rate instead of constantly overshooting.
fits.playstyle: Best in dense fields of soft, low-resistance rocks. Attach Okunis to widen and speed the window, then blitz the charge: the green fills almost instantly, so you tap-extract and move to the next rock fast. Without Okunis the narrow window is razor-thin, so reserve the bare head for confident experts and always carry the gadget for serious runs.
fits.bestFor: High-volume farming of soft common ore where rocks-per-hour beats per-rock value; experienced miners with steady throttle control.

ships.name: ARGO MOLE
ships.note: Group mining ship: THREE independently-operated S2 turret heads (one pilot + up to 3 laser operators). Each head is fitted separately — recommend a head+modules for ONE turret seat; the crew can mirror it or mix.

fits.tagline: Clean ore, forgiving window, steady fill — the all-day money head
fits.why: Yield = clean, valuable ore per fracture. The Impact II is the highest-throughput head in the eligible list and pairs two always-on passives in two of its three slots: Focus III adds Optimal Charge Window +40% for a very wide, forgiving green band, and FLTR-XL adds All Charge Rates 24 (faster fill) plus Inert Materials 24 reduction so far less worthless rock is scooped — directly raising the valuable fraction of every scan. The OptiMax gadget reduces loss of valuable resource on the deposit, squeezing maximum sellable mass from each rock. No instability is added by any of these passives, so the fit stays safe over long sessions.
fits.playstyle: Scan, attach OptiMax to the richest deposit, then sit comfortably in the wide green window and hold — the broad band means you rarely overshoot into overcharge. Pure passive, no active juggling, ideal for long group sessions.
fits.bestFor: High-value ore (Quantanium, Bexalite, Taranite) on medium-resistance rocks where you want the cleanest, most valuable refine per fracture.

fits.tagline: Crack the toughest deposits — raw power plus resistance-busting active and gadget
fits.why: Hard rocks need raw laser power plus resistance reduction. The Klein-S2 is the highest-power single-slot head in the eligible list, and its one slot takes the active Rime Module: Resistance -25% (and Shatter Damage -10%) — the strongest resistance cut available among modules — fired manually as you charge to drop how hard the rock pushes back. The Sabir gadget modulates the laser to make a tough deposit easier to crack and stacks with Rime, so the combined resistance drop lets that high power punch through dense rocks other heads stall on. The head's instability tradeoff is acceptable here: high-resistance rocks tend to fracture before they overcharge.
fits.playstyle: Attach Sabir to the stubborn rock, trigger Rime as you begin the charge to drop resistance, then push power hard to break it; re-trigger Rime each rock within its limited uses. Use on isolated hard rocks, not delicate clusters.
fits.bestFor: Dense, high-resistance C-type / E-type rocks and large hand-of-god boulders that won't break under normal power.

fits.tagline: Low instability, huge window, hard-to-blow-up training head
fits.why: Safety = low instability plus a big, forgiving window. The Arbor MH2 is the lowest-instability eligible head, giving the smallest overcharge-explosion risk. Both fitted modules are passive (zero active management for a new operator): Focus II adds Optimal Charge Window +37% and XTR adds another Window +15% plus a gentle All Charge Rates 5 and Inert Materials 5 — so the green band is enormous and easy to hold, and the fill never runs away. The Waveshift gadget keeps instability in check while enlarging the window further, making overcharge on a risky rock nearly impossible.
fits.playstyle: No active management — attach Waveshift on bigger or riskier rocks, hold the laser in the wide green band, release. Perfect first turret seat for a crewmate learning the mechanic.
fits.bestFor: New laser operators, volatile/high-instability rocks, and big risky deposits where losing the rock to overcharge is the main danger.

fits.tagline: Tight expert window, blistering fill rate — rocks down fast
fits.why: Speed comes from charge rate, paid for with a tighter window that experts can hold. The Lancet MH2 already runs a high optimal charge rate with low instability, but its small window makes it punishing solo. Torrent III piles on Optimal Charge Rate +40% (window only -1%) and FLTR-XL adds All Charge Rates 24 plus Inert Materials 24, so the window fills almost instantly for very fast fracture cycles while still keeping ore clean. To make that razor window survivable, the Okunis gadget increases the size and fill speed of the optimal charge window — restoring enough margin to actually hold the band at this speed. Net result: shortest time-per-rock of any eligible fit, for a skilled operator.
fits.playstyle: Attach Okunis to widen the otherwise tiny window, then ride the very fast fill with quick, precise releases — high APM, high reward. Best in experienced hands; pair it next to a beginner Arbor seat on the same Mole.
fits.bestFor: Experienced operators clearing dense fields of medium-resistance rocks fast, maximizing rocks-per-hour for the whole crew.

ships.name: Drake Golem
ships.note: Budget single-seat light-mining ship with a small ore hold. One swappable S1 head.

fits.tagline: Wide window, low inert, high throughput — the money fit
fits.why: Impact I is a 2-slot S1 head with strong throughput plus a built-in inert reduction and a comfortable window. Both slots are filled by passives that compound the yield goal: Focus III (+40% Optimal Charge Window) widens the green band so you sit in the sweet spot and overcharge less, while FLTR-XL adds All Charge Rates +24% (faster fill) AND Inert Materials +24% (less waste rock). OptiMax on the deposit further cuts inert, so each scan returns mostly sellable ore. Net: clean, high-value extraction with a big, forgiving window.
fits.playstyle: Scan for high-mass single rocks of medium resistance, attach OptiMax, charge into the wide green band and hold steady. The +24% charge rates fill quickly; the stacked inert reduction keeps the small ore hold full of sellable material.
fits.bestFor: Medium-resistance ores (Hadanite, Aphorite, Dolivine, lighter Quantanium rocks) where maximum sellable value per cycle matters on a small ore hold.

fits.tagline: Crack the tough stuff — resistance shredder
fits.why: High resistance is the wall on a budget S1 head, so this fit attacks it directly. Impact I gives the strongest raw power profile among the affordable 2-slot heads. Rime (active) applies Resistance -25% (the single biggest resistance cut), and Surge (active) stacks another Resistance -15% (accepting a minor Instability +10 during the crack) for a combined ~-40% resistance. Sabir on the deposit modulates power to make a tough rock crackable. Trigger Rime and Surge together at the charge to punch through rocks a bare laser can't break.
fits.playstyle: Attach Sabir to the high-resistance deposit, begin charging, then pop Rime (and Surge if still struggling) to collapse resistance for the few seconds needed to fracture it. Re-pop on the next rock — both are active modules with limited triggers, so manage uses.
fits.bestFor: Hard, high-resistance deposits (Riccite, Stileron, dense Quantanium nodes) that a stock S1 laser stalls on.

fits.tagline: Almost can't lose the rock — easy mode
fits.why: Arbor MH1 is the most forgiving S1 head by design, with strong instability reduction (calmer energy fluctuation, far less overcharge-explosion risk), a wide optimal window, and inert reduction so it stays profitable. Its single slot takes Optimum (active): Instability -10 and Overcharge Rate -80%, which nearly eliminates the runaway toward the danger zone. Waveshift on the deposit keeps instability in check while enlarging the optimal window. The result is an enormous, slow-to-overcharge sweet spot a new miner can hold comfortably.
fits.playstyle: Pick calm low/medium rocks, attach Waveshift, ease the laser into the very wide window and hold. If a rock feels jumpy, trigger Optimum to flatten the overcharge climb. Low stress, low loss.
fits.bestFor: First-time miners and low-to-medium instability rocks (inert-heavy clusters, basic Aphorite/Hadanite) where not losing the rock matters more than speed.

fits.tagline: Tight window, blazing charge — expert speed run
fits.why: Lancet MH1 is the speed head: high optimal charge rate with instability and inert reductions, paid for by a much smaller optimal window (a tiny, precise band for experts). Its single slot takes Torrent III (Optimal Charge Rate +40%, window only -1%), pushing combined charge rate very high while barely touching the already-tight window. To make the razor-thin window manageable, Okunis on the deposit increases the size and fill speed of the optimal window — restoring just enough forgiveness to land the ultra-fast charges. This is the fastest cycle-time fit on the ship for a skilled pilot.
fits.playstyle: Experts only: attach Okunis to widen the window slightly, then flash-charge each rock — the high charge rate fills the band almost instantly, so you tap-and-release for very short cycles and rack up many rocks per run.
fits.bestFor: Skilled miners farming dense fields of low-to-medium resistance rocks who want the shortest time-per-rock and high rocks-per-hour.

ships.name: Handheld Multi-Tool (Greycat OreBit / Pitman attachment)
ships.note: On-foot / FPS mining of small hand-sized deposits (e.g. in caves) using an S0 mining attachment head. No active modules/gadgets — head choice only.

fits.tagline: The widest, most forgiving green window on a hand laser
fits.why: The Lawson is the only eligible S0 handheld head whose built-in modifiers both point straight at ease-of-use. Optimal charge window +40% massively widens the green sweet-spot band, so it is far easier to hold the laser inside it without tipping into overcharge. Rock resistance -40% lets it crack deposits with much less power demand, so even at its modest 0.8 powerMax it breaks rocks the bare Arbor/Hofstede would struggle on. With no modules or gadgets allowed on this handheld, this window+resistance combo is the single safest head choice.
fits.playstyle: Charge gently and settle into the green; the +40% window gives a wide margin for error so you rarely overcharge. Ideal first hand-mining laser.
fits.bestFor: New miners and anyone clearing easy-to-moderate cave deposits with the lowest risk of losing the rock

fits.tagline: Most laser power plus highest throughput for the toughest hand deposits
fits.why: Hard rocks need raw power to overcome resistance, and the S0 Helix has the highest powerMax of all four eligible heads at 1.0 (vs 0.8 on the others) plus the top throughput at 5. With no modules or gadgets available to shave resistance on this platform, brute power is the only lever and the Helix delivers the most of it. The Lawson's -40% resistance soft-cracks well, but its 0.8 cap can stall on the densest shells, whereas the Helix's full 1.0 output muscles through them.
fits.playstyle: Point at the densest deposits, drive power up to crack the high-resistance shell, then ease into the window. Watch instability since this head has no calming modifier.
fits.bestFor: High-resistance hand-sized deposits that under-powered heads cannot break

fits.tagline: Calmest rock behaviour to land clean extractions without blowing the deposit
fits.why: For value mining the real enemy is overcharge: a violent rock that explodes destroys the ore. The Arbor MHV head's signature is instability reduction, which per the stat semantics directly tames the energy fluctuations that cause overcharge explosions, so far more rocks break intact and bank their full payout. Because handheld heads accept no inert- or yield-reducing modules, the most value-protective head is the one that most reliably keeps the deposit from blowing, and the calmest head does that. Its lower throughput (3) is the trade-off, but you are mining for yield retention, not speed.
fits.playstyle: Take it slow on twitchy or valuable rocks; the head's calmer instability response keeps the charge from spiking into the danger zone, so you bank gems and high-value pockets instead of gambling on speed.
fits.bestFor: Players prioritizing not losing valuable or unstable deposits, especially gem and high-value mineral pockets

fits.tagline: Solid power and throughput with a steady window for everyday cave runs
fits.why: The S00 Hofstede sits in the middle of the eligible heads: 0.8 powerMax and 4 throughput give it more material-processing speed than the Arbor (3) while it cracks the same easy-to-moderate deposits as the Lawson, without leaning hard into any single specialty. With no modules or gadgets to tune on a handheld, it is the safe default when you do not know what mix of rocks a cave holds, handling most small deposits competently rather than excelling only at the extremes.
fits.playstyle: Use it as your general-purpose hand laser: enough power and throughput for routine cave deposits, charge steadily and break each rock without needing to specialise the head to the rock type.
fits.bestFor: Miners who want one no-fuss head for mixed cave deposits rather than swapping for each rock type

ships.name: Greycat ROC / ROC-DS
ships.note: Ground vehicle gem mining. FIXED mining laser — you do NOT swap heads or modules. The "fit" here is technique + which gemstone deposits to target. ROC-DS is the dual-seat variant.

fits.tagline: Hadanite cluster harvest run
fits.why: The ROC laser fractures one node at a time, so income scales with how fast you clear high-value clusters rather than with gear. Hadanite is the top earner (~275 aUEC/unit), and on dense fields the deposits sit close enough that you barely move between them. Run the laser at full power to crack the node quickly, but feather the throttle the instant the deposit's energy bar climbs toward the red instability zone, then ease back in to keep the fracture inside the green optimal charge window. A clean fracture leaves loose chunks you scoop with the extraction beam at zero risk.
fits.playstyle: Park within ~30 m, hold fire, and watch the charge and instability bars. Pulse the laser to keep charge in the green band; if instability spikes, cut power for a beat then resume. After the node fractures, switch to extraction and vacuum every chunk before driving to the next deposit. Mind the cargo box: bank a full load and return to your ship or outpost rather than over-fracturing and leaving chunks behind.
fits.bestFor: Dense Hadanite surface clusters on Aberdeen (Hurston moon) and around HDMS-Norgaard, where deposits pack tightly with minimal downtime between spots.

fits.tagline: Aphorite + Dolivine sweep
fits.why: When you can't find pure Hadanite fields, mixed deposits keep the cargo box filling steadily. Aphorite (~152 aUEC) and Dolivine spawn in abundance alongside occasional Hadanite, so a methodical sweep beats hunting for the perfect node. These softer gems fracture with less power, so run the laser lower to avoid overshooting into instability and overcharging the deposit. The fixed laser has no overcharge or stability module to save you, so a blown node is lost income.
fits.playstyle: Drive a grid or perimeter pattern across the deposit field so you don't double-back. Use moderate laser power, watch for the fracture to pop, and grab everything. Triage by value when the box gets tight: prioritize any Hadanite, then Aphorite, then Dolivine. On the ROC-DS, have the second seat run extraction while the driver lines up the next node to nearly double throughput.
fits.bestFor: HDMS-Anderson (Hurston) and similar sites with abundant Aphorite/Dolivine clusters mixed with Hadanite; good steady-income runs when prime Hadanite fields are picked clean.

fits.tagline: Low-power cave & learner clusters
fits.why: The single biggest mistake new ROC pilots make is dumping full power and overshooting into the instability zone, which fractures violently and scatters or destroys the gems. Because the laser is fixed with no stability or overcharge modules to compensate, technique is your only safety margin. Running at reduced power widens your reaction time: the charge bar rises slowly, you learn to read the green optimal window, and you fracture cleanly almost every time. Slower, but you keep what you mine.
fits.playstyle: Set laser power around half, approach the node head-on so the beam stays centered, and gently increase power only if charge stalls. Never chase the red zone. In caves, stop the vehicle fully and mind your footing and clearance before firing; recover deposits in tight pockets one at a time. ROC-DS crews should let a new pilot drive while an experienced partner calls out the charge state until the rhythm clicks.
fits.bestFor: First ROC runs, cave-pocket gem deposits, and any single isolated node where a botched fracture would waste the trip. Practice on cheaper Aphorite/Dolivine before committing to high-value Hadanite.

ships.name: Argo ATLS GEO
ships.note: Mining exosuit (GEO variant of the ATLS cargo suit). FIXED extraction tool — no head/module swapping. "Fit" = technique + target deposits.

fits.tagline: Low-power surface clusters - learn the suit without wasting rocks
fits.why: The GEO's bespoke arm laser is strong enough to overcharge and shatter small hand-mineable gem rocks if fired on full power. Because this is a FIXED tool there is nothing to swap - no Focus/Optimum/Stampede modules and no Okunis/Waveshift gadgets are available to widen the window or tame instability. The only variable you control is laser power. Dropping to LOW power widens the effective extraction window and slows the approach to overcharge, which is exactly what a beginner needs to avoid popping small rocks and losing the ore.
fits.playstyle: Land near a surface deposit field on a moon. Approach a small rock on foot, set the laser to LOW power (Left Alt + Scroll down), and feather the trigger - aim for steady extraction rather than a hard fracture. Watch the instability bar; if it climbs fast, ease off the trigger to let it settle. Many small surface mineables yield directly without a full crack. Tractor the freed material into the 10 cSCU backpack and cycle rock to rock; the detachable backpack means trips back to a terminal are rare.
fits.bestFor: Small surface hand-mineables on moons (Aberdeen, Wala, Daymar-type terrain): low-tier loose gem rocks and small clusters. Ideal for the first 1-2 hours in the suit, before chasing high-value gems.

fits.tagline: Hadanite cliff-run - ping, jump-jet to ledges, low-power harvest
fits.why: Hadanite is the high-value gem, and the GEO's real edge over the wheeled ROC is its jump jets - it reaches clusters perched on cliffside ledges and cave mouths that no ground vehicle can touch. This is a technique loadout, not a gear loadout: the fixed laser cannot mount any module or gadget, so value comes purely from cluster-cycling speed and throttle discipline. Keep the laser on LOW power because Hadanite rocks are small and overcharge easily; a forgiving window matters more than raw beam power here.
fits.playstyle: Fly your transport low and slow over likely terrain and pulse Ping (TAB) repeatedly to surface deposit icons. Set down above a cluster, then jump-jet down onto the ledge or cave mouth. Switch the laser to LOW power, harvest each rock with short controlled pulses, and tractor the gems into the backpack - prioritise the highest-value gems in the cluster first, then top off with whatever else is reachable. Keep the loop tight: scan, drop, harvest, repeat. Rough community estimates put a backpack of mostly high-value gems in the tens of thousands of aUEC, but exact payouts and per-hour rates vary heavily with spawn luck and current prices, so treat any specific figure as approximate.
fits.bestFor: High-value gem clusters (Hadanite and similar) on moon cliffs, ledges and cave mouths - especially spots unreachable by the wheeled ROC. Mix in lower-tier gems from the same cluster to fill the backpack.

fits.tagline: Reach deposits a ROC can't drive to - within the suit laser's limits
fits.why: The honest niche for this suit is access, not crushing power. The GEO's jump jets let you reach medium deposits wedged in canyon shelves and cave pockets that a wheeled ROC cannot drive into. Be realistic about the tool: the GEO's fixed hand-tool-class laser is meaningfully weaker than a vehicle ROC mining laser and is NOT a reliable cracker of true ROC-class hard rocks. Crucially, this is a fixed-laser suit - you cannot bolt on any resistance-reducing module (Rime/Surge) or gadget (Sabir) to help break high-resistance rock, so there is no way to gear into genuine hard-rock cracking on this platform. Stick to deposits the laser can actually handle and use the suit's mobility for the rocks others skip.
fits.playstyle: Use jump jets to reach tight spots - cave mouths, canyon ledges, deep pockets. On a medium rock, run HIGH power (Left Alt + Scroll up) to build charge toward the fracture threshold, then watch instability closely so you crack rather than overcharge and lose it. After it fractures, drop to LOW power to clean up the chunks and tractor everything into the backpack. If a rock's resistance is clearly too high to fracture before it overcharges, leave it - without modules/gadgets there is no fix, and the suit is better spent on reachable medium deposits.
fits.bestFor: Medium, reachable deposits in jump-jet-only terrain - canyon shelves and cave pockets that wheeled miners skip. NOT for genuinely high-resistance ROC-class hard rocks, which this fixed laser cannot reliably crack.
